coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N, F co-doped carbon dots with tunable light emission for multicolor bio-labeling and antibacterial applications
coredata.dc:creator: Hua, Jianhao
Hua, Peng
Qin, Kunhao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as potential biomaterials for bioimaging and antimicrobial applications. However, the lack of tunable long-wavelength emission performance and imprecise antibacterial mechanism limit their practical application. Thus, developing versatile CDs that combine outstanding optical performance and excellent antibacterial activity is of great practical significance. Herein, we prepared a novel nitrogen and fluorine co-doped CDs (N, F-CDs) from o-phenylenediamine and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid, which exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield of 52.2%, large Stokes shift of 112 nm, as well tunable multicolor emission light from blue to red region. Thanks to the high biocompatibility and excellent photostability, the N, F-CDs were successfully implemented to multicolor biolabeling of mammalian cells, protozoan cells and plant cells. Moreover, the negatively charged N, F-CDs hold inherent efficient antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). By thoroughly studying the underlying antibacterial mechanisms at the molecular level through real-time quantitative PCR assay, we found the expression of related genes was notably down-regulated, further demonstrated that N, F-CDs against two bacterial strains had distinct target pathways. Our work provides a new reference for developing highly fluorescent multicolor CDs, and may facilitate the design and application of CDs-based nanomaterials in biological environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multifunctional carbon dots
Tunable emission
Multicolor bio-labeling
Antibacterial activity
Antimicrobial mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and optical properties of N, F-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Kunming University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079 ), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the design and synthesis of F-CDs, N-CDs and N, F-CDs. (b) Comparison of FL QY of three prepared CDs. (c) FL excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the N, F-CDs. (d) FL emission spectra of N, F-CDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 500 nm. (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs and N, F-CDs. Insets: photographs of N-CDs and N, F-CDs solution under daylight (left) and a 365 nm UV lamp (right), respectively. (f) FL lifetime decay curve of N, F-CDs.

Structural characterizations of CDs. The typical TEM images of (a) N-CDs and (b) N, F-CDs. HR-TEM images of (c) N-CDs and (d) N, F-CDs. (e) Raman spectra of N-CDs and N, F-CDs powders. (f) XRD pattern of N, F-CDs.
(a) Full survey XPS spectra of N-CDs and N, F-CDs. High-resolution XPS N1s (b) and F1s (c) spectra of N, F-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs and N, F-CDs.
Confocal fluorescence images of U-2 OS cells, HT-29 cells, and HeLa cells treated with N, F-CDs (80 μg mL−1) for 2 h, the scale bar is 20 µm.

(a) CLSM images of P. caudatum stained with N, F-CDs (80 μg mL−1) for 2 h. (b) Visual labeling of the flow route of food vacuoles in the cytoplasm of P. caudatum. (c) CLSM images of monolayer onion cells stained with N, F-CDs (80 μg mL−1) for 2 h.

The in vitro antibacterial activity of N, F-CDs. Growth curves of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus exposed to different concentrations of N, F-CDs in LB medium. (c) LB agar plates of E. coli and S. aureus treated with N, F-CDs for 6 h at different concentrations. (d) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus cells after treatment with or without N, F-CDs (scale bar is 1 µm).
CLSM images of (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus incubated with N, F-CDs (30 μg mL−1) at 20, 40, and 60 min, the scale bar is 10 µm.
Quantitative analysis of relative expression level of related genes in (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus treated with N, F-CDs.
Schematic diagram of antibacterial mechanisms of N, F-CDs against E. coli (left) and S. aureus (right) at molecular level.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N, F co-doped carbon dots with tunable light emission for multicolor bio-labeling and antibacterial applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Elemento-Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Elemento-Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Elemento-Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Energy Materials Chemistry (Ministry of Education), Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, Yunnan Province 650500, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province, Kunming, Yunnan Province, 650011, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Third People's Hospital of Yunnan Province, Kunming, Yunnan Province, 650011, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as potential biomaterials for bioimaging and antimicrobial applications. However, the lack of tunable long-wavelength emission performance and imprecise antibacterial mechanism limit their practical application. Thus, developing versatile CDs that combine outstanding optical performance and excellent antibacterial activity is of great practical significance. Herein, we prepared a novel nitrogen and fluorine co-doped CDs (N, F-CDs) from o-phenylenediamine and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid, which exhibit high fluorescence quantum yield of 52.2%, large Stokes shift of 112 nm, as well tunable multicolor emission light from blue to red region. Thanks to the high biocompatibility and excellent photostability, the N, F-CDs were successfully implemented to multicolor biolabeling of mammalian cells, protozoan cells and plant cells. Moreover, the negatively charged N, F-CDs hold inherent efficient antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). By thoroughly studying the underlying antibacterial mechanisms at the molecular level through real-time quantitative PCR assay, we found the expression of related genes was notably down-regulated, further demonstrated that N, F-CDs against two bacterial strains had distinct target pathways. Our work provides a new reference for developing highly fluorescent multicolor CDs, and may facilitate the design and application of CDs-based nanomaterials in biological environment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have sparked keen research interest in the past decade. Owing to their comprehensive advantages, such as low cost, favorable chemical stability, good biocompatibility, abundant surface groups, excellent water solubility, CDs are regarded as versatile nanomaterials with broad application prospects in various fields [1–3]. Particularly, the distinctive fluorescence (FL) emission and optical features of CDs endow them as promising biomedical agents for applications in photomedicine and bioimaging [4,5]. Nevertheless, despite such encouraging advancements, the major shortcoming of most reported CDs stem from the insufficient short-wavelength emission from blue to green region and lack of multicolor emission, which hinders the development of CDs, particularly in biological environment relevant fields [6,7]. Moreover, fluorescence quantum yield (FL QY) is one of important parameters to evaluate the optical performance of CDs. However, compared with highly efficient blue/green CDs, long-wavelength emissive CDs always suffer from undesirable low FL QY, while challenges remain in the preparation of long-wavelength and multicolor emissive CDs with high FL QY [8,9].

In order to meet the practical application requirements in the field of human health, numerous studies have been conducted to prepare desirable CDs with long-wavelength or tunable emissions from blue to red region, and further apply them in various biotechnologies [10,11]. To date, diverse multicolor emissive CDs were obtained through “bottom-up” approach by selecting efficacious precursors and regulating reaction conditions [12–14]. Among various selected precursors, thanks to the electron-rich arene structure, as well appearance of graphitic nitrogen, phenylenediamines isomers are commonly employed to afford multicolor nitrogen (N)-doped CDs (N-CDs) [4,10,15]. Furthermore, considering the manipulability of core graphitization degree and content of heteroatoms, multicomponent synthesis combining phenylenediamines with other molecules facilitate the implementation of straightforward co-doping strategies and regulation of optical performance [8,16–18]. Interestingly, contrary to the electron donor of N-group, due to the inherent electron-accepting group with the high electronegativity and electron-withdrawing tendency, doping with halogen atoms, especially fluorine (F) atom, has been demonstrated to be a reliable strategy for tuning FL emission and improving FL QY [19–21]. However, we note that attempts to develop tunable multicolor CDs using phenylenediamine and organic F dopants as precursors are scarce. Therefore, the development of an effective co-doping strategy to prepare tunable multicolor CDs using π-extended F-containing precursors is still highly desirable.

Beyond these, several studies have proven that F-doped CDs (F-CDs) have good biocompatibility and high affinity to cell membrane, which is favorable for their biomedical utilization [20,22]. More importantly, many reported self-functional F-CDs derived from quinolone antibiotics presented effective antibacterial activity, which retained the antibacterial bioactivities features of the precursors [23,24]. These results inspired us to employ highly fluorescent tunable multicolor F-CDs as a potential antibacterial biomaterial. Unfortunately, although CDs have been extensively investigated to defeat bacterial infections [25–27], their biomedical applications were still limited by some drawbacks. On the one hand, many reported positively charged CDs tend to adhere on the targeted bacterial surface via electrostatic interaction and then exert antibacterial effect, however, they can cause undesired cytotoxicity and hemolysis against red blood cells since most biological membranes are negatively charged [28]. On the other hand, most of the antibacterial mechanisms of CDs mainly involve physical/mechanical destruction, reactive oxygen species (ROS)-related oxidative stress, photothermal/photocatalytic effect [25,29,30]. These explorations are inspiring, but cannot further explain the molecular mechanism of antibacterial effect of CDs. An in-depth understanding of the signaling pathway of antibacterial CDs against bacteria is essentially needed to guide the precise design of antibacterial CDs.

To address the above issues, we adopted o-phenylenediamine (OPDA) and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid as precursors to prepare a novel N and F co-doped CDs (N, F-CDs) by a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained N, F-CDs exhibited high FL QY, large Stokes shift of 112 nm, as well remarkably tunable multicolor FL emission, we can readily modulate multicolor emission light from blue to red region by varying different excitation wavelengths. Encouraged by the merits of wavelength-tunable and high biocompatibility, the N, F-CDs were successfully applied to multicolor biolabeling of mammalian cells, protozoan cells and plant cells. Notably, the negatively charged N, F-CDs exhibited intrinsic antibacterial activity against Gram-negative (G-) Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive (G+) Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus). We thoroughly investigated the underlying antimicrobial mechanisms at the molecular level by real-time quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR) assay, and further demonstrated that N, F-CDs against E. coli and S. aureus had different target pathways, which provides an in-depth understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying the interaction between N, F-CDs and bacteria.

The chemicals, characterization, and other experimental details, including cell cytotoxicity assay, bacteria culture, protozoan and plant cells imaging, RNA extraction and cDNA synthesis can be found in the Electronic
Supplementary Material.

The N, F-CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, OPDA (0.2 g) and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid (0.2 g) were mixed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol, respectively. The precursor solution after 10 min ultrasonic dissolving was then transferred into Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (100 mL) and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the obtained crude product solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm filtration membrane, and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove insoluble impurities. Then, the supernatant was decanted carefully from centrifuge tubes and residual solvent ethanol was removed by vacuum rotary evaporation at 55 ℃. Subsequently, the collected solid N, F-CDs powder was redispersed in ultrapure water. The N, F-CDs aqueous solution was then purified by dialyzing against ultrapure water for 48 h through a 1000 Da dialysis bag to remove remaining precursors. Finally, purified solid N, F-CDs powder was obtained through vacuum freeze-drying.
In addition, N-CDs and F-CDs were synthesized using 0.2 g OPDA or 0.2 g 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid as a single-component precursor through a similar process, respectively.

U-2 OS cells, HT-29 cells, and HeLa cells were seeded in confocal-specific glass culture dishes and cultured at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h, respectively. Then, they were incubated with N, F-CDs suspension with the same concentration (80 μg mL−1) for 2 h, the cells were washed with PBS buffer and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 20 min at room temperature, and then washed twice with PBS buffer. Finally, the fluorescent imaging was performed by CLSM system. In addition, HeLa cells was also incubated with various concentrations of N, F-CDs (20, 40, 80 and 150 μg mL−1) for 2 h, and imaged by CLSM system.

G- E. coli (BW 25113) and G+ S. aureus (ATCC 25923) were used as representative bacterial strains, the in vitro antibacterial activity of N, F-CDs was evaluated by growth curve method and agar plate dilution method. For the growth curves of both bacteria strains, the overnight cultures were inoculated in fresh liquid LB medium, and then incubated together with different doses of N, F-CDs (5, 30, and 60 μg mL−1). Control groups without N, F-CDs were performed in the same way. The bacterial growth was estimated by monitoring the optical density of bacterial suspension at 600 nm (OD600) every 1 h. For the agar plate method, after incubating with N, F-CDs at different concentrations (0, 5, 15, 30, and 60 μg mL−1) for 6 h, the bacterial suspension was diluted 103-fold in LB and then applied to agar plates, respectively. After 12 h of incubation at 37 °C, bacterial colonies grown on all solid agar plates were visible. To examine the effect of N, F-CDs on the morphology and surface structure of bacterial cells, both bacteria strains were incubated with N, F-CDs at a final concentration of 60 μg mL−1 for 6 h, the treated bacteria were then observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

E. coli and S. aureus were inoculated into fresh liquid LB medium and grown at 37 °C to mid-log phase (OD600 = 0.6). 2 mL bacteria suspension was harvested, bacterial cells were washed thrice with sterile PBS via centrifugation (10,000 g for 5 min) and then resuspended in 1 mL PBS solution. After that, they were incubated at 37 °C with N, F-CDs at a final concentration of 30 μg mL−1, both bacteria strains were stained for different times (20, 40, and 60 min) and then washed with sterile PBS. Finally, the fluorescence imaging was observed by CLSM. As a supplementary control group, G+ Bacillus cereus (B. cereus) was also stained and imaged through the same process.

The relative expression level of related genes of both bacteria strains was determined through RT-qPCR assay. In brief, related genes of E. coli (waaC, ftsA, ftsZ, rpoA, rpoB, csrA, ptsH, ptsI, holA, dnaE, and waaA) and S. aureus (icaA, agrA, ftsA, polA, dnaE, rpoB, and rpoA) were selected and studied by a LineGene 9600 Plus real-time PCR detection system. The gene-specific primer sequences for RT-qPCR in this study are detailed in Table S1 and Table S2. RT-qPCR reaction conditions adopted were: 5 min at 95 °C for denaturation, followed by 40 amplification cycles consisting of 95 °C for 20 s, 55 °C for 20 s, and 72 °C for 20 s. Melting curve analysis was performed from 60 °C to 95 °C with an increment of 1 °C for 20 s. The expression level of waaA and rpoA was adopted as the endogenous control for E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. Finally, the expression level of all selected genes was calculated by the 2− ΔΔCt method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optical properties of N, F-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three different CDs, including F-CDs, N-CDs and N, F-CDs, were easily prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method using anhydrous ethanol as the reaction solvent. To obtain multiple heteroatoms co-doped N, F-CDs, OPDA and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid were reasonably utilized as reaction precursors ( Fig. 1a). It is common knowledge that the FL QY and optical performance of CDs are determined by the reaction conditions. It is difficult to obtain multicolor emissive CDs only through solvothermal treatment of OPDA using pure water as reaction solvent [16]. Thus, based on the fact that solvent has a remarkable effect on FL regulations of CDs [11], we attempt to use anhydrous ethanol as solvothermal media. Additionally, reaction time is directly related to the degree of polymerization and carbonization of precursors, and the reactant ratio of OPDA to fluorine dopant is crucial for controlling the heteroatoms content in CDs. Therefore, for improving FL QY and tuning the emission color of desired CDs, we optimized the above reaction conditions: the reaction time of 12 h (Fig. S1a) and the feed rate of 0.2 g precursors (Fig. S1b) were determined as the optimal conditions.

The optical properties of N, F-CDs were thoroughly investigated. As expected, the prepared N, F-CDs exhibit strong FL emission accompanied by a 52.2% FL QY, which is dramatically higher than that of F-CDs (8.8%) and N-CDs (40.1%) (Fig. 1b), the result indicates that N and F co-doping could greatly improve FL QY of CDs. Notably, the comparisons between our N, F-CDs with published F-CDs are shown Table S3, such FL QY is outstanding among previously reported F-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1c, both the excitation and emission spectra of N, F-CDs are distinct and well-defined, the optimal excitation wavelength is around 428 nm, and corresponding emission band is centered at 540 nm. Here, of interest to us is that the remarkable large Stokes shift of 112 nm, which is superior to that of F-CDs (Fig. S2a) and N-CDs (Fig. S2b). The quite small overlap between excitation and emission spectra leads to few self-absorption, which is beneficial for efficient FL emission [13]. Significantly, the most important and distinctive optical feature of N, F-CDs is its tunable multicolor FL emission. As shown in Fig. 1d, the FL emission spectra gradually shifted from blue to red when the excitation wavelength was varied from 340 nm to 500 nm. After normalization, the redshifted FL spectra were well-defined and cover the most of the visible region under varied excitation, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of all spectra still remains stable and uniform. This excellent performance is quite different from the prepared F-CDs and N-CDs (Fig. S2c-d), as well widely reported CDs whose excitation-dependent FL emission properties. It can be considered a new breakthrough in optical performance for traditional blue/green emissive CDs. For the N-CDs, clear aqueous solution with brown color was observed under daylight, but only emits relatively weak green-yellow light under UV irradiation (insets in Fig. 1e), while F-CDs emit even weaker blue FL (Fig. S3a). In contrast, transparent N, F-CDs water dispersion exhibits brighter yellow FL color under a 365 nm UV lamp, which is an obvious signature of the highly fluorescent emission. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1e) of N, F-CDs exhibits significant absorption at 224 nm and 276 nm, corresponding to the π-π * transition of CC bond in the aromatic sp
2 domains and the defect states n-π * transition of CO/CN bonds [8], respectively. Compared with the N-CDs, the broad absorption band at around 350–480 nm decreased significantly while that at 276 nm increased greatly, it could be attributed to the consumption of C-NH2 from OPDA and more formation of CO/CN in the polymerization and carbonization process [31]. Meanwhile, the weaker absorption band in the lower-energy region can be assigned to the surface states containing heteroatom cluster-rich structures [32]. Based on the above characterization, it can be speculated that the tunable multicolor emission properties of N, F-CDs was related to the multiple electronic absorption transitions and electronic band gap. We further explored the exciton recombination dynamics of N, F-CDs, the FL decay curve was well fitted by a single exponential decay function, with FL lifetime calculated as 2.66 ns (Fig. 1f). Different from the bi-exponential decay mode of F-CDs and N-CDs (Fig. S3b), the monoexponential decay characteristic indicates that radiative decay is extremely dominant with a minimal nonradiative emission channels, which is beneficial for efficient FL emission [33].

In order to further analyze the special optical behavior and structural characteristics of N, F-CDs, we investigated the structure, composition, and functional groups in detail. The morphologies and structures of F-CDs, N-CDs and N, F-CDs were first characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 2a-b, both N-CDs and N, F-CDs were well-dispersed, homogeneous spherical particles, with average diameters of about 5.5 nm (Fig. S4a) and 2.5 nm (Fig. S4b), respectively. Obviously, the TEM data demonstrate that the redshift of FL from N-CDs to N, F-CDs is not due to quantum size effect. Their high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images (Fig. 2c-d) show well-resolved crystalline lattice fringes corresponding to the (100) planes of graphite carbon, indicating that both N-CDs and N, F-CDs contain graphite-like structure [34]. Differently, the F-CDs with an average diameter of 3.4 nm are amorphous (Fig. S5). The graphitization degree of both N-CDs and N, F-CDs was also reflected by Raman spectra. Two typical bands were peaked at 1362.3 cm−1 and 1579.5 cm−1 (Fig. 2e), referring to the defects or disordered structures (D band) and graphitic structures (G band) in the N, F-CDs frameworks, respectively. The intensity ratios (I
D
/I
G ) are 0.934 and 0.919 for N-CDs and N, F-CDs, respectively. The decreased value and stronger G band indicate a higher graphitized content of N, F-CDs, suggesting an increased size of conjugated sp
2 carbon cores [12]. Generally, OPDA could condense to form OPDA dimer, trimer or polymer, and these intermediates further produce N-CDs through the horizontal and vertical polymerization, carbonization [31,35]. Interestingly, the average size of N, F-CDs was substantially reduced with the introduction of F dopant in the synthesis, while the graphitization degree was strengthened. Therefore, we speculate that OPDA and 2,3,5,6-tetrafluoroterephthalic acid may react to produce intermediates since the -COOH groups of F dopant are highly functional. The intermediates further grow and then produce desired N, F-CD, the F dopant does not destroy the formation of graphitic carbon nuclei, but inhibits the growth of small dots into larger carbon particles [8,36]. Based on the above characterization analysis, we further characterized the structure of N, F-CDs by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 2f, the distinct diffraction peak at 20.4° is attributed to the characteristic graphitic structure with (002) interlayer spacing of 0.43 nm, which is larger than the normal spacing of 0.35 nm of graphitic (002) crystal plane. The enlarged interlayer distance may be resulted from steric hindrance of functional groups on the layer surface or edge. We also found a broad peak at around 40.7°, signifying graphitic (100) crystal plane with d spacing of 0.21 nm [9], which is well consistent with the high crystallinity degree of N, F-CDs reflected by HR-TEM result.

To further understand the chemical compositions and surface groups, we characterized the prepared CDs using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared (FT­IR) spectra. The full XPS spectra ( Fig. 3a) show that the prepared N, F-CDs consisted of C, N, O, and F elements, while the N-CDs only contain C, N, and O elements. This proves that the desired CDs are successfully doped by N and F. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S6a) shows several peaks at 283.1, 283.6, 284.3, 285.0, 285.7, and 286.9 eV, which can be assigned to CC, C-C, C-N, C-O, CO/CN, and C-F, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. S6b) exhibits three peaks at 530.2, 531.3, and 532.5 eV, corresponding to C-O, CO, and COOR groups, respectively. For the doped N and F components, N1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) reveals the presence of C-N-C (397.6 eV), N-(C)3 (398.8 eV), and amide/amino type -NH groups (399.9 eV). It has been reported that defect states formed by N-doping and surface amide/amino groups facilitate the high FL QY and excitation-dependent FL emission [34,37]. The emergent F1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) features two peaks corresponding to semi-ionic C-F (685.6 eV) and covalent C-F (686.5 eV), which facilitate the formation of hydrogen bonds with amide/amino N-type atoms [38]. As mentioned above, N and F co-doping brings higher FL QY for N, F-CDs. The presence of intra-dot and inter-dot hydrogen bonds expand the conjugated aromatic sp
2 domains. The incorporation of large conjugated domains, complex heteroatom clusters, and defect states formed by graphitic N doping produce new energy level in N, F-CDs, which can improve the utilization of photon. Meanwhile, the stronger absorption bands in ultraviolet region enhances the photon absorption and electronic transition, as well the monoexponential FL decay mode reduces the energy losses in FL emission progress, thus effectively improving the FL QY of N, F-CDs [8,19].

In addition, FT-IR spectra were also recorded, as shown in Fig. 3d, surface functional groups on N-CDs are similar to that of OPDA precursor molecules [39]. Compared with the N-CDs, the -NH2 stretching vibration (3304 and 3174 cm−1) and bending vibration (1640 cm−1) were significantly decreased, while remarkable changes can be seen for C-O/C-N stretching vibrations at 1409 and 1132 cm−1. It suggests that more -NH2 groups of OPDA may be converted into C-N-C structure during the formation processes of N, F-CDs. Moreover, the emergence of three peaks at 3437 cm−1, 1336 cm−1, and 1224 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of -OH, CO bond, and C-F bond, respectively. It can be attributed to the introduction of F dopant in the synthesis. We also found that benzene ring (1495 cm−1) and amino group (N-H bending at 1462 cm−1, and waging at 758 cm−1) still exist, suggesting part of residual functional groups of precursors may still be distributed on the surface of N, F-CDs [21,38,39]. Accordingly, the N, F-CDs have a good dispersibility and stability in water or ethanol due to containing abundant hydrophilic groups. Considering the effects of these surface groups, we measured the ζ potentials of prepared CDs. As expected, the ζ potential of N, F-CDs is negatively charged, and the value was measured to be − 27.7 mV (Fig. S7). These results are in good accordance with the above XPS results. Finally, the stability of N, F-CDs under various circumstances was investigated. The N, F-CDs show temperature independent FL and stable FL intensity within a wide pH range 5.0–11.0 (Fig. S8a-b). The FL intensity is also stable in the presence of various ions and high ionic strength (Fig. S8c-d), indicating that the N, F-CDs can be used under physiological conditions.

On the basis of above characterization analysis, the tunable FL emission of N, F-CDs can be ascribed to their large conjugated system and multiple energy gaps. The short-wavelength emission originated from broad energy gap state associating with aromatic sp
2 carbon domains. Moreover, N and F-doping introduced complex surface states, inter-dot and intra-dot hydrogen bonds facilitate the expanding of conjugated sp
2 domains, which effectively tune the narrower energy gaps. Thus, the long-wavelength emission can be obtained through lower energy gaps under matched excitation [19,40,41].

Motivated by the excellent optical properties, we further investigated the potential of N, F-CDs for multicolor bio-labeling application. Prior to biological application, MTT assays were conducted using U-2 OS cells, HT-29 cells, and HeLa cells, the results (Fig. S9) reveal that the N, F-CDs are almost nontoxic even at concentration of up to 200 μg mL−1. In the preliminary cell imaging experiment, three cell lines were co-incubated with N, F-CDs for 2 h and cell images were recorded. As presented in Fig. 4, all cells emit intense blue, green, and red FL signal under different excitation channels, as well the morphology of all cells remains unchanged, confirming low cytotoxicity and good in vitro biocompatibility of N, F-CDs. Afterward, HeLa cells was stained with different concentrations of N, F-CDs and imaged by CLSM system (Fig. S10). The intracellular FL intensity gradually enhanced with the increasing concentration of N, F-CDs, signifying the N, F-CDs effectively penetrated all of the incubated HeLa cells, regardless of the varied concentration. We also recorded the high-magnification CLSM images of single HeLa cell stained with 40 μg mL−1 N, F-CDs (Fig. S11). The bright FL signals under different channels are well merged with each other, and the region where N, F-CDs accumulated within cell indicates that the N, F-CDs not only can penetrate cell membranes, but even enter into cell nuclei. These results demonstrate the multicolor cell imaging capacity of N, F-CDs, even they can be engineered as drug carriers.

In addition to in vitro mammalian cells imaging, we further performed protozoan cells and plant cells labeling using N, F-CDs. P. caudatum was adopted as a model and co-incubated with N, F-CDs for 2 h before imaging. P. caudatum took up the N, F-CDs, and clear FL signal in cell interior can be observed under different excitation channels ( Fig. 5a), which show that N, F-CDs efficiently accumulated in organelles and nucleus. More interestingly, we recorded the flow route of food vacuoles in the cytoplasm. As presented in Fig. 5b, the enlarged image shows that oral groove and cytostome were not specifically stained by N, F-CDs, while the food vacuoles were clearly labeled, food bubbles moved in cell interior with the flow of cytoplasm, and the food in the bubbles was gradually digested and absorbed. The images indicate the excellent photostability of N, F-CDs during cell internalization. On the contrary, N, F-CDs mainly concentrated in cell membrane and nucleus in onion cells, with almost no FL signal in the cytoplasm after coincubation for 2 h (Fig. 5c). The images show that the onion cells still maintained good integrity after N, F-CDs penetration. These results confirm that N, F-CDs could be applied as a potential fluorescent reagent for in vitro multicolor bio-labeling.

Inspired by the effective antibacterial activity of F functionalized CDs [23,24], we evaluated the potential usages of prepared N, F-CDs as antibacterial biomaterial against two representative bacterial strains, i.e. E. coli and S. aureus. To this end, the general in vitro antibacterial activity was preliminarily evaluated by measuring the bacterial growth curves. As shown in Fig. 6a, the proliferation of E. coli was slightly inhibited in the presence of 5 μg mL−1 N, F-CDs. With the concentration of N, F-CDs increasing, the inhibition rate was remarkably strengthened. Upon co-treatment with 60 μg mL−1 N, F-CDs for 5 h, the treated bacteria did not grow, while the bacteria in control group were in rapid logarithmic growth. Similar results were also obtained against S. aureus (Fig. 6b). N, F-CDs possess a good inhibitory effect on the proliferation of S. aureus, there was no bacterial growth after exposure to 60 μg mL−1 treatment for 5 h. To verify the antibacterial capability of N, F-CDs, the viabilities of both bacterial strains were further assessed by plate counting method, it was also observed the dose-dependent antibacterial activity of N, F-CDs as expected (Fig. 6c). Compared with the control groups, the viabilities of both E. coli and S. aureus were significantly decreased as the concentration of N, F-CDs increasing, almost no bacterial colony was observed on the LB agar plates after treatment with 60 μg mL−1 N, F-CDs. The results are well consistent with the experimental observations of bacterial growth curves, which confirm that N, F-CDs hold excellent inhibitory effect on E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+).

In light of these findings, SEM characterization was conducted to visualize antibacterial effect on bacterial morphology (Fig. 6d). Surprisingly, the morphology of treated E. coli still maintained intact, typical rod shape and clear smooth surface was observed for all bacterial cells, which was similar to that of control group. However, the size of bacteria became apparently smaller, which is quite different from the most previous reports [30,42,43]. This observation excludes the possibility of physical damage on surfaces of E. coli cells [44], we speculate that bacterial division and metabolism may be suppressed due to the chemical effect of N, F-CDs. In contrast, compared with control group, the membrane of spherical S. aureus was severely damaged, the treated bacteria display broken structures and content leakage. Thus, the N, F-CDs may exert antibacterial effectiveness towards S. aureus through destroying cellular integrity based on various possible physical/chemical interactions. These results further reflect that N, F-CDs inhibit proliferation of both E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+) through different mechanisms.

However, restricted by the resolution limit, the conventional SEM images fail to yield more information on the binding and adhesion between N, F-CDs and bacteria. Here, on the basis of the excellent multicolor labeling performance of N, F-CDs, we adopted CLSM imaging system to intuitively monitor the binding between N, F-CDs and bacterial cell. As shown in Fig. 7a, clear FL signal within the E. coli cells can be easily visualized after coincubation for 20 min, indicating an efficient internalization of N, F-CDs into bacteria. The intracellular FL signal kept stable and bright at different time points, suggesting the good stability of N, F-CDs as antibacterial agent within E. coli cells. In contrast, FL signal was barely found from the treated S. aureus after 20 min coincubation (Fig. 7b), with the time elapsing to 40 min, only weak FL signal appeared, and the FL intensity within bacteria became stronger after 60 min. This trend demonstrates a relatively low binding efficiency between N, F-CDs and S. aureus. Such phenomenon may be attributed to the difference in membrane structures and composition between G+ and G- bacteria [28,45]. S. aureus possesses thicker cell wall than E. coli, and cell wall of S. aureus has a thick peptidoglycan layer with negatively charged polymeric teichoic acids, which retards the internalization efficiency [46]. To verify our speculation, we further adopted B. cereus (G+) as supplement, and performed bacterial cell labeling under the same conditions. Likewise, intracellular FL intensity gradually appeared and enhanced as the coincubation time prolonging (Fig. S12). It reflects a time-dependent uptake process of N, F-CDs into G+ bacteria, which is well-consistent with that of S. aureus. Therefore, the challenge caused by electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged N, F-CDs and bacterial cell wall is negligible. Taken together, it is evident that prepared N, F-CDs hold intrinsic excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli (G-) and S. aureus (G+).

In order to have a deeper understanding on the antibacterial mechanism of N, F-CDs, according to the above SEM observations, we examined the expression level of related genes of both bacteria strains through RT-qPCR assay. The expression analysis of related genes in E. coli and control groups were presented in Fig. 8a, heptosyltransferase WaaC encoded by waaC gene catalyzes the synthesis of lipopolysaccharides (LPS), which also known as endotoxins, is a key component of outer membrane hydrophobic lipid bilayer of G- bacteria [46,47]. Thus, waaC gene plays an important regulatory role in the synthesis of G- bacterial cell membrane, negligible expression difference was observed here, indicating that N, F-CDs do not interfere the biosynthesis of LPS and cell membrane in E. coli. The FtsA and FtsZ protein encoded by ftsA and ftsZ genes are indispensable for bacterial cell division, the interaction between FtsZ and other Fts protein regulates bacterial cell division and cell morphology, among them, interaction between FtsA and FtsZ is essential for the correct division of pathogenic E. coli [48]. Obviously, the expression of ftsA and ftsZ genes was down-regulated 2.18-fold and 3.27-fold, respectively, suggesting N, F-CDs effectively inhibit the asexual binary fission proliferation of E. coli. RNA is essential for genetic information transmission, which is synthesized through RNA polymerase (RNAP) catalysis, and the rpoA and rpoB genes encode a core subtype of RNAP in E. coli [49]. Notably, the expression of rpoA and rpoB genes was down-regulated 6.29-fold and 5.29-fold, respectively. This indicates that N, F-CDs severely inhibit the synthesis of RNAP, then interfere the transmission of genetic information, leading to the degeneration of other biological functions in E. coli.

In addition, the uptake and metabolism of energy provide a material basis for bacterial life activities (such as proliferation and growth). As a conditionally essential gene in E. coli, carbon storage regulator A (csrA) gene encodes the CsrA protein, as well controls the entire metabolic network. Meanwhile, it’s also closely associated with the various life activities, such as central carbon metabolism, bacterial motility, quorum sensing (QS), and pathogenicity in E. coli [50]. Moreover, phosphotransferase system (PTS) is the main pathway through which a series of saccharides are absorbed into the bacterial cytoplasm, while PTsGlc is the specific pathway for glucose, which was mainly encoded by ptsH and ptsI genes in E. coli [51]. As disclosed, the expression of the csrA, ptsH and ptsI genes were significantly down-regulated 3.48-fold, 2.03-fold, and 4-fold, respectively, implying that the energy uptake and metabolism of E. coli are notably hindered after treatment with N, F-CDs. Besides, ignorable difference was observed for holA and dnaE genes, which are involved in the and DNA replication, suggesting the process of DNA semiconservative replication was not disrupted by N, F-CDs [52].

As for S. aureus, the relative expression levels of related genes were presented in Fig. 8b. S. aureus is capable of forming biofilms, a complex multi-cellular structure on the surfaces. The biofilms protect the cells from antimicrobial agents, as well facilitate signal transmission and nutrient exchange within microbial communities. The expression level of polysaccharide intercellular adhesin (PIA) is closely associated with the capability of biofilms formation in S. aureus, while the synthesis of PIA is regulated by the icaA gene. Thus, icaA is considered a key gene for biofilms formation in S. aureus [53]. Additionally, accessory gene regulator (agr) of S. aureus also acts as an essential regulator for biofilms formation [54]. More importantly, the agr is closely related with the QS system of S. aureus, which is involved in the biological activities of microbial communities, such as signal transduction, specific gene expression, and antibiotic resistance. As depicted in Fig. S13, agrA gene is located at a critical position of the agr operon sequences in the QS agr system [55]. The expression level of icaA and agrA were notably down-regulated 4.24-fold and 2.24-fold, respectively, indicating that N, F-CDs effectively suppress the biofilms formation, as well interfere signal transmission within QS agr system.

The FtsA protein encoded by ftsA gene is indispensable for S. aureus bacterial division, and bacterial cells are unable to maintain structural integrity in its absence [56]. It can be observed that the expression of ftsA was down-regulated 1.96-fold. Besides, two DNA polymerases, PolA and DnaE, encoded by the polA and dnaE genes, are involved in chromosomal DNA replication in S. aureus. Notably, the expression of indispensable genes (polA and dnaE) related to the DNA replication was down-regulated 2.18-fold and 3.45-fold, respectively. These results imply that bacterial division and DNA replication of S. aureus are severely disrupted upon treatment with N, F-CDs.

On the basis of above analyses, we further clarified that prepared N, F-CDs against both G- and G+ bacteria exhibit distinct target pathways, and underlying antibacterial mechanism was illustrated in Scheme 1. For the E. coli (G-), N, F-CDs mainly impede bacterial asexual binary fission proliferation, interfere the transmission of genetic information, weaken the energy uptake and metabolism, then resulting in bacterial growth arrest and cell death. Dissimilarly, N, F-CDs functioned as a QS inhibitor in S. aureus (G+) to disrupt the QS system and suppress biofilm formation, impede the DNA replication and cell division, thus causing bacteria rupture. Considering the advantages of ultrasmall size, negative surface charge, and great dispersity in aqueous solution, N, F-CDs are potentially useful for the application in combating bacterial pollution.

In this work, a novel negatively charged N, F-CDs with high FL QY was rationally designed and synthesized. The N, F-CDs exhibit tunable multicolor emission from blue to red region due to their large conjugated systems and multiple energy gaps. They can serve as potent fluorescent reagent for in vitro multicolor biolabeling of mammalian cells, protozoan cells, and plant cells based on the excellent biocompatibility and photostability. Moreover, the N, F-CDs possess intrinsic excellent antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus through different target pathways. Importantly, the underlying antibacterial mechanisms were investigated at the molecular level via RT-qPCR, the N, F-CDs achieved antibacterial effect towards E. coli mainly involve inhibition of asexual binary fission proliferation and transmission of genetic information, suppression of energy uptake and metabolism. For S. aureus, N, F-CDs functioned as a QS inhibitor to interfere with the QS system and suppress biofilm formation, impede the DNA replication and cell division. This work will offer references to exploit versatile CDs and design negatively charged nanomaterials for diverse biomedical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been used for bioimaging and antimicrobial, which is significant for the development of environmental management and human health. However, some drawbacks, such as insufficient optical properties, undesired cytotoxicity, and ambiguous harmful effects limit their applications in biological environments. Herein, we prepared a novel N, F-CDs with multicolor emission and efficient antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus, which is beneficial for disease diagnosis and combating bacterial pollution. The molecular mechanism of antibacterial effect was thoroughly investigated, it may facilitate the design and application CDs-based antibacterial biomaterials.

Jianhao Hua: Conceptualization, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization. Peng Hua: Validation, Resources, Supervision, Project administration. Kunhao Qin: Methodology, Software, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2017M20162118079), and the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2018M643535).
